doc.title: Allocate the transaction price
doc.event: Rae Co. collects $100,000 upfront on a contract bundling equipment (standalone price $75,000), installation ($15,000), and two years of service ($30,000). Equipment and installation are completed this period. Prepare the entries for signing, delivery, and year 1 of service.

variants.label: Ratio split, then recognize each obligation on its own clock

lines.label: full contract price
lines.why: Cash timing and revenue timing are strangers in this chapter — collecting everything today earns nothing today.
lines.trap: Signing a contract alone is NOT a revenue event. The credit is a liability until promises are kept.

lines.label: a contract liability
lines.why: The obligation to deliver three promises — released PO by PO as each is satisfied.
lines.trap: Kieso also calls this 'Contract Liability' — same account, either name earns the point.

entries.caption: Equipment delivered + installation completed (point in time)

lines.label: release two POs
lines.why: Equipment's slice (62,500) + installation's slice (12,500) release together when both are satisfied.
lines.trap: The slices come from the ALLOCATION (62,500 / 12,500), not the standalone prices (75,000 / 15,000) — the discount spreads proportionally.

lines.label: equipment's allocated 62,500
lines.trap: Recognizing the full 75,000 standalone price ignores that the bundle sold at a discount.

lines.label: installation's allocated 12,500
lines.trap: Each PO carries its own proportional share of the bundle discount.

lines.label: half the service slice
lines.why: Service's allocation is 25,000 (100,000 × 30/120), recognized ratably: 12,500 per year.
lines.trap: Over-time obligations release on the SERVICE clock, not the cash clock — the money's been in hand since day one.

lines.label: earned as time passes
lines.why: Straight-line is the default measure for stand-ready service.
lines.trap: At any balance-sheet date, the remaining Unearned Revenue = the unserved months' slice — a favorite exam ask.

computationPaths.narration: Relative standalone split: total standalone 75 + 15 + 30 = 120. Each PO gets contract price × (its standalone ÷ 120). The 20,000 bundle discount spreads proportionally — nobody's slice escapes it.

memorize.body: Allocation = transaction price × (PO's standalone price ÷ total standalone prices). Standalone price unknown? Estimate: adjusted market, expected cost + margin, or residual (last resort).
Third time you've met this ratio machine: lump-sum stock sales, bonds-with-warrants, now performance obligations. One skill, three chapters of points.

Contract MODIFICATIONS: new distinct goods at standalone prices → treat as a separate contract; otherwise reallocate the remaining price prospectively (or cumulative catch-up if not distinct). Know the fork exists.

questions.prompt: A 100,000 contract bundles POs with standalone prices 75,000 / 15,000 / 30,000. Revenue recognized when the equipment (75,000 standalone) is delivered =

distractors.feedback: 75,000 is the STANDALONE price. The bundle sold at a discount (100 vs 120), and every PO absorbs its share: 100,000 × 75/120 = 62,500.
The full price covers THREE promises — only equipment's allocated slice (62,500) is earned at delivery; installation and service release on their own clocks.

build.scaffold: Ratio first (each standalone ÷ 120, times 100,000), entries second. Signing = cash vs liability only; each delivery releases its allocated slice — never its standalone price.
